title: GPT-5.5 Drops, DeepSeek V4 Shakes Up Pricing, Google Unveils TPU 8
summary: La voix est synthétique, le propos reste authentique. Désormais narré par la voix IA de votre obligé. Version anglaise également disponible, mon clone force un peu sur l'accent, l'original s'en sort mieux. OpenAI released GPT-5.5 on Thursday, topping benchmarks but raising API costs by roughly 20 percent after token inflation. DeepSeek launched V4-Pro and V4-Flash on Friday, offering 1 million token context at a fraction of frontier model pricing and claiming near-parity with GPT-5.4 on coding tasks. Google announced TPU 8t and TPU 8i at Cloud Next on Wednesday, purpose-built chips designed to handle training and inference for agentic AI workloads at scale.

script:
Votre Revue de Presse IA hebdo — semaine du 26 avril 2026: Model Wars Heat Up.

This Friday brought the sharpest pricing contrast yet in the frontier model race. OpenAI's GPT-5.5 landed Thursday evening, claiming the top spot on multiple benchmarks but carrying a roughly 20 percent net price increase over GPT-5.4 due to token inflation. Less than 24 hours later, DeepSeek dropped V4-Pro and V4-Flash in preview, offering 1 million token context windows and near-frontier performance at one-fifth the cost of GPT-5.5. Meanwhile Google used its Cloud Next conference to unveil TPU 8t and TPU 8i, two specialized chips designed to power the next wave of agentic workloads. In today's episode: Friday's model launches and their immediate market implications, the hardware moves positioning cloud providers for the agent era, and the off-radar signals that didn't make the tech headlines. Then we'll look at what to watch next week, including earnings calls that will test whether AI infrastructure spending is translating into revenue.

OpenAI released GPT-5.5, codenamed Spud, on Thursday evening. The model tops Artificial Analysis's Intelligence Index with 60 points, three ahead of Claude Opus 4.7 and Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview, which are tied at 57. API pricing is set at 5 dollars per million input tokens and 30 dollars per million output tokens, matching GPT-5.4's nominal rates. However, the model uses a new tokenizer that inflates token counts by roughly 40 percent on average, pushing the effective cost increase to around 20 percent. Artificial Analysis reports that GPT-5.5 consumes about 40 percent fewer tokens per request than GPT-5.4, bringing the net price hike down to approximately 20 percent. OpenAI says the model delivers stronger performance on multi-step workflows and agentic tasks, with response speed matching GPT-5.4 in real-world use. The model is available in ChatGPT and Codex for paid subscribers, with API access rolling out once additional cybersecurity guardrails are in place. Co-founder Greg Brockman described it as a new class of intelligence and a big step toward more agentic and intuitive computing.

DeepSeek released preview versions of V4-Pro and V4-Flash on Friday, marking the first major update since V3.2 in December 2025 and R1 in January 2026. V4-Pro is a 1.6 trillion parameter mixture-of-experts model with 49 billion active parameters, making it the largest open-weights model available, more than double the size of DeepSeek V3.2 at 685 billion parameters. V4-Flash has 284 billion total parameters with 13 billion active. Both models support 1 million token context windows natively and are released under the MIT license. DeepSeek claims V4-Pro achieves performance comparable to GPT-5.4 on coding competition benchmarks and nearly closes the gap with current leading models on reasoning tasks, though it trails frontier models slightly on knowledge tests, suggesting a developmental lag of approximately three to six months. API pricing is 1.74 dollars per million input tokens and 3.48 dollars per million output tokens for V4-Pro, and 0.14 dollars input and 0.28 dollars output for V4-Flash, making V4-Pro the cheapest of the larger frontier models and V4-Flash cheaper than OpenAI's GPT-5.4 Nano. The models are available via DeepSeek's API, website, and app, with weights on Hugging Face and ModelScope.

Google announced TPU 8t and TPU 8i at Cloud Next on Wednesday, the eighth generation of its Tensor Processing Unit line and the first to split into two specialized chips. TPU 8t is optimized for training and can run complex models on a single massive pool of memory, with updated server clusters housing 9,600 chips and two petabytes of shared high-bandwidth memory. Google claims TPU 8t can scale linearly to up to one million chips in a single logical cluster, delivering 121 FP4 exaflops of compute per pod, nearly three times higher than the previous Ironwood generation. The company reports a goodpute rate of 97 percent, meaning less waiting and wasted effort, with better handling of irregular memory access, automatic fault management, and real-time telemetry across all connected chips. TPU 8i is designed specifically for inference, enabling AI agents to complete multi-step workflows quickly. Google says the new TPUs, combined with its full-stack infrastructure including networking, data centers, and energy-efficient operations, create the underlying engine to bring highly responsive agentic AI to the masses. The announcement came as Google wrapped up Cloud Next, with AWS timing a Meta deal for millions of Graviton CPUs to coincide with the close of the conference.

Meta signed a deal with AWS on Friday to use millions of Graviton chips to power its growing AI needs. The Graviton is an ARM-based CPU, not a GPU, and AWS's latest version was designed specifically to handle AI-related compute needs. The deal brings more of Meta's cash back to AWS instead of competitors like Google Cloud. Last August, Meta signed a six-year, 10 billion dollar deal with Google Cloud, though Meta had until then primarily been an AWS customer that also used Microsoft Azure. AWS timed the announcement of this deal right as the Google Cloud Next conference wrapped up. Agents create compute-intensive workloads like real-time reasoning, writing code, search, and the coordination involved in managing agents through multi-step tasks, and AWS says Graviton was designed to handle these workloads. Anthropic had already swooped in with a deal announced earlier this month that commandeered many of AWS's Trainium chips for years to come. The Claude maker agreed to spend 100 billion dollars over 10 years to run its workloads on AWS, with a particular focus on Trainium, while Amazon agreed to invest another 5 billion dollars, bringing its total to 13 billion dollars of investment into Anthropic in return.

NVIDIA and Google Cloud announced expanded collaborations on Monday to bring agentic AI to the center of enterprise marketing operations across creative production and customer experience orchestration. The partnership brings together Adobe's creative and customer experience platforms and the new Adobe CX Enterprise Coworker, WPP's global media and marketing expertise, and NVIDIA's accelerated computing and software stack, including NVIDIA Nemotron open models, NVIDIA Agent Toolkit, and the NVIDIA OpenShell secure runtime for building and running secure agentic AI systems. Powered by the NVIDIA OpenShell runtime, every agent operates within a secure, isolated environment, delivering enterprise-grade control, consistency, and auditability across the entire marketing lifecycle, with verifiable policy management. The announcement highlighted the need for intelligent systems that can plan, create, produce, and activate content continuously without compromising control, governance, or brand integrity. Consider a global retailer delivering the right offer, image, copy, and price across millions of product, audience, and channel combinations, updated in minutes instead of months. For marketing and creative teams, that means moving from one-size-fits-all campaigns to tailored experiences that are always on, always relevant, and on brand.

Anthropic filed a new court document on Tuesday arguing it has no way to control or shut down its AI models once they're deployed by the Pentagon. The company says it has no visibility, technical ability, or any kind of kill switch for its technology once it's deployed, and that the Pentagon has the opportunity to test models before deployment. The filing was submitted to a federal appeals court in D.C. as part of Anthropic's lawsuit challenging the Pentagon's designation of the company as a supply chain risk. The Pentagon contends Anthropic is inappropriately getting involved in how its technology can be used in sensitive military operations. Anthropic's usage policies include no Claude for autonomous weapons or mass surveillance, red lines that the Pentagon dismissed as red herrings. The company argues that once models are deployed in classified settings, it has no technical means to enforce those policies or intervene in their use. The case highlights the tension between AI companies' stated ethical guidelines and the practical realities of deploying models in environments where the provider has no operational control or oversight.

Cohere and Aleph Alpha announced on Thursday that they intend to merge, with the transaction backed by Schwarz Group, Germany's largest retailer. Schwarz Group plans to lead a funding round into the combined company by making a 600 million dollar structured financing commitment. CNBC reported that the Series E deal, which is also expected to draw other investors, will close later this year. Toronto-based Cohere has raised about 1.6 billion dollars in funding from NVIDIA and other backers since launching in 2019. The company offers several AI model families, each optimized for a different set of use cases, with its most capable model, Command A Reasoning, supporting prompts with up to 256,000 tokens and a tool use feature that can carry out work in business applications. Aleph Alpha launched in 2019, the same year as Cohere, and develops custom AI models for organizations in highly regulated sectors such as finance and healthcare. Many of Aleph Alpha's customers are public sector organizations in the European Union. The merger reflects a broader consolidation trend in the AI startup landscape as companies seek scale and resources to compete with larger players.

xAI launched grok-voice-think-fast-1.0 on Friday, a new flagship voice model that tops the tau-voice Bench at 67.3 percent, outperforming Gemini 3.1 Flash Live at 43.8 percent, Grok Voice Fast 1.0 at 38.3 percent, and GPT Realtime 1.5 at 35.3 percent. The model is available via the xAI API and is already deployed at scale powering Starlink's live phone operations. It is evaluated as a full-duplex voice agent, meaning the system processes incoming speech and generates responses simultaneously rather than waiting for the speaker to stop before it begins thinking. This is how humans communicate in real conversations and why handling interruptions is a genuinely hard technical problem: the model must decide in real time whether a mid-sentence utterance is a correction, a clarification, or just a filler word, and adjust its behavior accordingly. The tau-voice Bench evaluates agents specifically under realistic conditions: noise, accents, interruptions, and natural turn-taking, making it a more relevant measure for production deployments than traditional clean-audio ASR benchmarks. The model is purpose-built for complex, ambiguous, multi-step workflows across customer support, sales, and enterprise applications.

Verkor.io, an AI chip design startup, announced on Wednesday that it has designed a RISC-V CPU core entirely using an agentic AI system. The CPU, dubbed VerCore, has a clock speed of 1.5 gigahertz and performance similar to a 2011-era laptop CPU. The company's key claim is that this approach is more effective than using only specialized AI systems for specialized tasks within the overall design process. Suresh Krishna, cofounder at Verkor.io, says the team learned that the better approach is to let the AI agent solve the whole problem. Verkor.io's agentic system is called Design Conductor, and it's not itself an AI model but a harness for large language models. A harness is software that forces an AI agent to proceed through structured steps, in this case steps like those a team of human chip architects would follow: design, implementation, testing, and so on. The harness also manages subagents and a database of related files, meaning it can work autonomously with only an initial prompt from the user. It outputs a Graphic Design System II file, which can be used in existing electronic design automation software. Synopsys and Cadence, two major players in EDA software, also have agentic AI tools, but Design Conductor is different because it's built to handle chip design from spec to completion with full autonomy.

Anthropic's Mythos Preview model, announced two weeks ago, can autonomously find and weaponize software vulnerabilities, turning them into working exploits without expert guidance. These were vulnerabilities in key software like operating systems and internet infrastructure that thousands of software developers working on those systems failed to find. This capability will have major security implications, compromising the devices and services we use every day. As a result, Anthropic is not releasing the model to the general public, but instead to a limited number of companies. The news rocked the internet security community, with few details in Anthropic's announcement angering many observers. Some speculate that Anthropic doesn't have the GPUs to run the thing and that cybersecurity was the excuse to limit its release. Others argue Anthropic is holding to their AI safety mission. Finding vulnerabilities in source code is the type of task that today's large language models excel at, and though the evaluation of LLMs encompasses various domains within the realm of natural language processing, limited attention has been paid to probing their linguistic capability of understanding contextual features. The question is how we adapt to it, and whether an AI that can hack autonomously will create permanent asymmetry between offense and defense.

GitHub announced changes to Copilot Individual plans on Tuesday, including tightening usage limits, pausing signups for individual plans, restricting Claude Opus 4.7 to the more expensive 39 dollar per month Pro+ plan, and dropping the previous Opus models entirely. The key paragraph from the announcement states that agentic workflows have fundamentally changed Copilot's compute demands, with long-running, parallelized sessions now regularly consuming far more resources than the original plan structure was built to support. As Copilot's agentic capabilities have expanded rapidly, agents are doing more work, and more customers are hitting usage limits designed to maintain service reliability. It's easy to forget that just six months ago heavy LLM users were burning an order of magnitude less tokens. Coding agents consume a lot of compute. Copilot was also unique among agents in charging per-request, not per-token, meaning that single agentic requests which burn more tokens cut directly into their margins. The most recent pricing scheme addresses that with token-based usage limits on a per-session and weekly basis. The announcement does not clearly clarify which product called GitHub Copilot is affected by these changes, though judging by the linked GitHub Copilot plans page this covers Copilot CLI, Copilot cloud agent and code review features on GitHub.com itself, and the Copilot IDE features available in VS Code, Zed, JetBrains, and more.

OpenAI announced Codex Labs on Tuesday and partnerships with top global systems integrators to bring Codex to thousands of engineering organizations. The company shared that more than 4 million developers are using Codex every week, up from 3 million in early April. Beyond individual adoption, enterprises are moving quickly to roll Codex into real workflows across engineering and beyond. Companies are using Codex across the software development lifecycle. Virgin Atlantic is using it to increase test coverage and increase team velocity, reducing technical debt and improving performance. Ramp is using it to accelerate code review. Notion is using it to quickly ship features. OpenAI is working to take market share from Anthropic's enterprise business by engaging several consulting partners to help enterprises deploy and scale Codex. Partners will get early access to AI tools in hopes that they can help enterprises rethink their business processes in the age of AI in a different way, according to chief revenue officer Denise Dresser. It's part of OpenAI's broader shift toward enterprise revenue, after Claude Code's mass adoption led to businesses spending more money with Anthropic than OpenAI in recent months.

Anthropic is hitting turbulence at a critical moment, with a cascade of challenges converging ahead of a potential IPO that could value the company near 800 billion dollars. The AI darling, whose revenue has tripled to 30 billion dollars this year on the back of its wildly popular coding tools, has never been more valuable or more vulnerable. Chief rival OpenAI senses opportunity in the Claude maker's recent stumbles, courting frustrated developers and pitching itself as the steadier alternative ahead of dueling IPOs. Anthropic's problems over the past two months span nearly every part of its business: product quality, pricing, security, and capacity, and are starting to compound. Perceived declines in Opus 4.6 performance triggered an initial wave of user complaints, followed by a controversial pricing change for Claude Code that was announced then reversed within hours. Security concerns emerged after reports that 24,000 fraudulent accounts from Chinese AI firms were used to generate 16 million exchanges with Claude. Capacity constraints have led to intermittent service disruptions, and the company has struggled to communicate clearly about changes to its product lineup and pricing structure. The confluence of issues is testing Anthropic's reputation for reliability at a moment when enterprise customers are making long-term platform decisions.

Côté déploiements. Ulta Beauty launched agentic commerce across Google surfaces on Wednesday, including AI Mode in Search and the Gemini app, making the Ulta Beauty assortment shoppable within these surfaces. With this agentic commerce experience, shoppers can receive product recommendations, compare options, and complete checkout for eligible purchases within Google's conversational interfaces. The experience is powered by Universal Commerce Protocol, the open standard for agentic commerce. Ulta Beauty also launched Ulta AI, a new AI shopping assistant now available on Ulta.com and soon to be added to the Ulta Beauty App. Ulta AI leverages insights from the beauty retailer's 46 million members to provide shoppers with personalized guidance as they navigate the digital assortment. This AI shopping assistant was built with Gemini Enterprise for Customer Experience. Ulta Beauty said in March that AI contributed to the retailer's better-than-planned financial performance in the fourth quarter, with AI in Ulta Beauty's automated marketing engine adding to the company's ability to deliver personalized experiences at scale.

Home Depot deployed AI voice agents in an early pilot, with results showing the system identifies customer intent in roughly 10 seconds and delivers solutions four times faster than traditional menu-based systems. It can initiate service requests, send product links, and help customers complete purchases directly from the call. It can also assemble a shopping cart based on a customer's verbal description of a project. The deployment reflects a sustained pivot toward digital engagement and AI-assisted servicing visible across bank earnings through the past few weeks. At Wells Fargo, the company's AI-powered assistant Fargo has already surpassed one billion customer interactions three years after launch, underscoring the scale of self-service adoption. That level of engagement indicates that customers are increasingly comfortable resolving issues without speaking to a human agent. Bank of America reported similar trends, with digital engagement metrics climbing quarter over quarter. The same pattern is visible in retail, where AI is moving call centers away from scripted menus and toward conversational problem-solving that can complete transactions in real time.

Revolut trained a proprietary model called PRAGMA on 40 billion transactions, app interactions, and financial events drawn from 25 million users across 111 countries. It doesn't answer questions or write emails. It makes decisions: who's likely to commit fraud, who's a credit risk, which customers are about to leave. According to Revolut, PRAGMA outperforms every task-specific system the company previously ran. Mastercard announced in March that it's building a generative AI model on hundreds of billions of anonymized payment transactions, with applications spanning fraud, loyalty, and portfolio analytics. Plaid introduced a transaction model built to standardize how merchant data is read across institutions. PRAGMA sits apart from that group in two ways. Mastercard and Visa operate as networks: they see transactions, but not the full arc of a customer's financial life. Plaid sees account data, but its model targets a specific problem, merchant name standardization, rather than a broad decisioning layer. Revolut, as a neobank, holds the complete picture. It sees where customers spend, what they trade, how they move money internationally, and how they navigate the app before and after each transaction. PRAGMA is trained on all of it, fused into a single representation of each user.

Siemens introduced the Eigen Engineering Agent on Tuesday, an AI system designed to plan and validate automation engineering tasks in operational environments. The system uses multi-step reasoning and self-correction to carry out tasks autonomously and operates directly inside engineering platforms, letting it complete workflows from initial design through to validation. The agent is designed to interpret project requirements, generate automation code, configure industrial systems, and refine outputs until predefined performance targets are achieved. This includes tasks like programmable logic controller programming, human-machine interface setup, and device configuration. It is built to meet industrial requirements for correctness and reliability during execution. The system connects to Siemens' Totally Integrated Automation Engineering platform, TIA Portal, letting it access project-specific data like structures and component relationships. In pilot deployments involving more than 100 companies in 19 countries, the Siemens system was applied to standard automation engineering processes. Participating organizations included ANDRITZ Metals, CASMT, and Prism Systems. Prism Systems used the system to generate automation code, configure industrial systems, and refine outputs until predefined performance targets were achieved.

AppZen launched AP Inbox Service Center on Monday, a new accounts payable service that deploys eight prebuilt artificial intelligence agents to automate how finance teams handle vendor email. The new service adds agents to AppZen Inbox and converts the product into a structured service center where every inbound vendor message becomes a trackable, auditable request with an assigned status, owner, and resolution path. AppZen argues that while invoice processing has seen heavy automation investment over the past decade, the vendor-facing communication layer has remained largely manual. Payment status inquiries, bank change requests, duplicate invoice resolution, vendor statement reconciliation, W-9 submissions, and remittance requests still require team members to read each email, research records, draft responses, and route them to the right person. AppZen customer data indicates AP reviewers spend as much as one week a month on that work. The eight agents handle a specific scenario end-to-end, from reading the message and looking up records in the enterprise resource planning system to evaluating the request against organizational policy and taking action. Every decision is auditable, showing what the agent evaluated, what it found, and why it acted.

Portal26 launched Agentic Token Controls on Wednesday, a new module designed to rein in runaway token consumption by autonomous AI agents. The new module lets administrators set token budgets for individual agents, specific workflows, or the whole organization. Agents nearing a cap get throttled, and those that blow through one can be paused or killed outright. Portal26 argues that multistep autonomous agents built on large language models can unintentionally enter recursive loops, over-query systems, or expand tasks beyond their original scope, leading to exponential token usage and surprise bills. The company is pitching the new module as the first dedicated tool to manage the risk at enterprise scale. Chief Executive Arti Raman cited Uber Technologies as an example of an enterprise that discovered adoption speed and cost predictability are on a collision course, and said the new module gives customers telemetry to scale agents without waking up to an invoice they didn't plan for. Alongside the enforcement layer, the module provides real-time visibility into where and how tokens are being spent across agentic systems, with adaptive safeguards that intervene automatically as limits are approached.

Alibaba Group Holding struck its first external partnership for its flagship consumer artificial intelligence app on Thursday, linking it with China Eastern Airlines in a move that pushes its agentic capabilities beyond the company's own ecosystem and into real-world services. The company said the fresh collaboration allowed users of the Qwen app to manage the full flight booking process, from search and ticket purchase to seat selection and check-in, within a single natural-language chat interface. It also acted as a proactive intelligent companion, anticipating user needs and suggesting options. The Qwen app, Alibaba said, would expand into airline loyalty services and other travel-related offerings, adding partners both within and beyond Alibaba's ecosystem to deepen real-world use cases and strengthen its position in applied AI. The deal underscores the e-commerce and AI giant's effort to push forward adoption of its AI stack, spanning infrastructure, cloud services, foundation models, and both enterprise and consumer-facing applications, to justify its heavy investments. Alibaba in early 2025 pledged to invest 380 billion yuan, or 55.6 billion dollars, in AI and cloud over three years, and has already spent more than 120 billion yuan in the sector in the four quarters through September.

Snowflake expanded its Snowflake Intelligence and Cortex Code offerings on Tuesday in the hope of bringing users deploying and developing artificial intelligence inside the Snowflake portfolio. Snowflake Intelligence is framed as a tool for generalized business users, while Cortex Code is destined for developers and technical teams' desks. A press release from the company lists additional features on both platforms, including an increase in the number of integrations they have with third-party software. It also details new automation features and simpler, web-based methods of building agentic AI workflows. Snowflake Intelligence, aimed at non-technical staff, is among the platforms on the market today that advertise an ability to execute tasks inside existing business workflows. Users can describe to the LLM what they'd like to see happen in natural language, and it executes given tasks. Snowflake lists preparing presentations, running multi-step analyses, and sending follow-up messages as some of the uses it envisages. Data can be drawn from an organization's internal and linked digital assets, including structured and unstructured data, with external sources connected by various protocols and pre-built connectors. New interfaces using Model Context Protocol are available, and the company has named the Google business suite, Jira, and Salesforce, including Slack, as among the systems Snowflake Intelligence can now interface with.

Sous le radar. Yann LeCun left his post as chief AI scientist at Meta late last year and founded Advanced Machine Intelligence Labs, or AMI Labs, which raised one billion dollars in startup funding for a company that employs 12 people. LeCun believes that the breed of technology we currently term AI, large language models, is not the way through which it will develop meaningful and long-term results. LeCun asserts that AMI Labs will remain a research organization not expected to produce a saleable product for maybe five years. The team at AMI Labs are concentrating not on huge, general-purpose language-based models, but AIs that comprise collections of modular components, trained for and operating in specific use-cases. LeCun's proposed system of artificial intelligence would comprise a world model specific to the domain in which the AI would operate, an actor that proposes steps to take next based on classical reinforcement learning, a critic that analyses the different options drawn from the world model and based on short-term memory and assesses the proposed steps according to hard-coded rules, a perception system that would be specific to the AI's use, a short-term memory, and a configurator that would orchestrate the movement of information between each of the above. Unlike large language models that have been trained on only one source of information, the text scraped from the internet, each instance of LeCun's AI would be given directed data relevant only to their environment and purpose.

China's State Grid Corporation earmarked 6.8 billion yuan, or 1 billion dollars, for the procurement of embodied intelligence, or artificial intelligence-enabled robots, to operate vital infrastructure. The firm intends to purchase around 8,500 robots this year, according to an internal company development plan seen by Jiemian. When similar plans by other Chinese utility firms including China Southern Power Grid are taken into account, industry insiders expect total investment on embodied intelligence in the sector to surpass 10 billion yuan in 2026. The firm will also deploy an army of humanoid and dual-arm robots to perform more high-stakes tasks, including maintaining the country's rapidly expanding ultra-high-voltage power grid. About 5.8 billion yuan of State Grid's investment will go towards procuring hardware, with the remainder allocated to research and development and staff training. The deployment reflects China's rapid scaling up of the use of AI-powered robots to operate vital infrastructure, with the country's main grid operator unveiling a blockbuster plan to deploy thousands of bots to handle a range of roles, from inspecting remote substations to performing maintenance on ultra-high-voltage power lines.

Apple researchers released ParaRNN, a new framework for parallelized RNN training that achieves a 665 times speedup over the traditional sequential approach. This efficiency gain enables the training of the first 7-billion-parameter classical RNNs that can achieve language modeling performance competitive with transformers. Recurrent Neural Networks are naturally suited to efficient inference, requiring far less memory and compute than attention-based architectures, but the sequential nature of their computation has historically made it impractical to scale up RNNs to billions of parameters. The computational cost of the attention mechanism in a transformer grows quadratically with sequence length, whereas the computation required for a single forward pass through an RNN is the same regardless of how much context came before. This enables constant-time token generation during inference, making them particularly attractive for efficient deployment. But there's a catch: this efficiency advantage only applies at inference time. Unlike transformers, RNN training can't be parallelized along the sequence length. The very property that makes RNN efficient at inference, their sequential, recurrent structure, becomes a fundamental bottleneck during training. To accelerate research in efficient sequence modeling and enable researchers and practitioners to explore new nonlinear RNN models at scale, the ParaRNN codebase has been released as an open-source framework for automatic training-parallelization of nonlinear RNNs.

A new benchmark from Handshake AI and McGill University called BankerToolBench tests AI agents against the typical workflows of junior investment bankers. After running nine current top models through the test, the verdict from the bankers involved is blunt: none of the outputs are fit for client use. The team enlisted around 500 current and former investment bankers from firms including Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan, Evercore, Morgan Stanley, and Lazard. Of those, 172 designed the tasks themselves, logging more than 5,700 hours of work. Each of the 100 tasks took a human banker an average of five hours, with some running up to 21 hours. BankerToolBench grades the actual deliverables a junior banker would hand to a supervisor: Excel financial models with working formulas, PowerPoint decks for client meetings, PDF reports, and Word memos. The agents have to dig through data rooms, pull from market data platforms like FactSet and Capital IQ, and parse SEC filings. A single task can trigger up to 539 calls to the language model, with 97 percent tied to tool use or code execution. Each deliverable is checked against a banker-designed rubric averaging 150 individual criteria. The criteria span six areas, including technical correctness, client readiness, compliance, auditability, and consistency across files. Grading is handled by an AI verifier the authors built called Gandalf, based on Gemini 3 Flash Preview. It agrees with human reviewers 88.2 percent of the time, slightly above the 84.6 percent agreement rate between two human reviewers. GPT-5.4 leads, but no model produced a single output rated ready for client delivery.

A Federal Reserve Board study analyzing monthly US employment data from a large household survey found that programmer job growth has nearly halved since ChatGPT launched in November 2022. Before ChatGPT launched, programming-heavy jobs in the US were growing at just under 5 percent a year, well above the overall labor market. Since then, that pace has dropped sharply. In sectors with an especially high share of programmers, like IT services and software development, growth has essentially flatlined. To strip out industry-wide effects, the researchers built a counterfactual employment curve showing how many programmers there would be if their share within each industry had stayed constant and only the size of the industries had changed. Even after this adjustment, programmer employment is still falling by about three percentage points a year. A control test using occupations barely touched by AI shows no comparable dip. Stretched over three years, the gap works out to roughly 500,000 jobs that probably would have existed without the rise of large language models. But the authors strongly caution against reading this number as a straight count of lost jobs. Many would-be programmers likely found work in adjacent fields. AI may be reshuffling tasks across job categories, with programming skills now embedded in roles that weren't traditionally classified as programmer positions.

Ce qu'il faut surveiller la semaine prochaine. Tesla reported Q1 revenue of 22.4 billion dollars on Tuesday, up 16 percent from a year earlier, with net income totaling 477 million dollars, up 17 percent, but operating expenses ballooned 37 percent to 3.78 billion dollars. Its operating margin fell to 4.2 percent, declining sequentially for the second consecutive quarter. CEO Elon Musk acknowledged on an earnings call that the company is planning on a very significant increase in capital expenditures, but he said it will be well justified for a substantially increased future revenue stream. The company is investing heavily in the development and production of humanoid robots, self-driving cars, and AI chips. Watch for how the market interprets the widening gap between AI infrastructure spending and near-term profitability, a dynamic that will play out across multiple earnings calls next week. Several cloud providers and chip manufacturers are scheduled to report, and analysts will be looking for evidence that the massive capital expenditures on AI infrastructure are translating into revenue growth and margin expansion.

President Trump signed an executive order in December targeting state AI laws, with three provisions due on March 11 that have not been completed or publicly announced. The Federal Trade Commission was supposed to issue guidance on how consumer protection law applies to AI models, including when it could override state laws that alter their truthful outputs. The Commerce Department was due to review and publish an evaluation of state AI laws. The Department of Justice was expected to provide legal analysis on federal preemption of state AI regulations. The missed deadlines are raising doubts about how forcefully the administration can follow through on its push to rein in states on AI regulation. Watch for whether these deliverables surface next week, and if not, whether the administration provides an updated timeline or signals a shift in priorities. The executive order was seen as a major test of the administration's willingness to assert federal authority over state-level AI governance, and the delay suggests either capacity constraints or internal disagreement over the approach.

The White House accused China of industrial-scale theft of US artificial intelligence technology in a memo seen by the Financial Times on Thursday, with the warning that the government will crack down on this practice. The US government has information indicating that foreign entities, principally based in China, are engaged in deliberate, industrial-scale campaigns to distill US frontier AI systems, wrote Michael Kratsios, director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy. The accusation marks an escalation in tensions around Chinese companies allegedly using American AI research in the race to dominate the technology, and is happening ahead of a meeting between the countries' presidents. The issue came to attention when China's DeepSeek was accused of using distillation, or training smaller AI models based on the output of larger ones, to develop a powerful product at a lower cost. Kratsios' memo said the Trump administration would share information with American AI companies about attempts by foreign actors to conduct unauthorized, industrial-scale distillation and help them prevent attacks. He added that Chinese campaigns were leveraging tens of thousands of proxy accounts to evade detection and using jailbreaking techniques to expose proprietary information. Watch for whether the administration follows through with concrete enforcement actions, and whether the accusations affect the upcoming presidential meeting or lead to new export controls or sanctions.

The UK government quietly published revised data this week showing that energy use by AI datacentres in the UK could cause the emission of up to 123 million tonnes of carbon dioxide over the next 10 years, about as much as generated by 2.7 million people. That latest figure replaces a previous estimate, since deleted, that claimed emissions would reach a maximum of 0.142 million tonnes of CO2 in a single year, an increase by a factor of more than 100. The latest estimates were revealed in a revision to the UK compute roadmap, which sets out the government's plan to build a world-class compute ecosystem for delivering artificial intelligence in the UK, a goal on which the government has staked its hopes for economic growth. According to the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology's latest estimates, the carbon impact of the planned AI buildout could range from 34 million to 123 million tonnes of CO2, about 0.9 percent to 3.4 percent of the UK's projected total emissions between 2025 and 2035. The lower range of the estimate would depend on greater efficiency in AI models and hardware, and faster decarbonisation of the UK's energy grid. Watch for whether other governments revise their own AI infrastructure emissions estimates upward, and whether the revised figures affect public support for AI datacenter buildouts or lead to new regulatory requirements for energy efficiency and carbon offsets.

Supply chain cracks are constraining the AI boom, with the Iran war threatening to squeeze the industrial inputs that chip manufacturers depend on. The AI buildout is the latest to be throttled by this trend, in this case the effects of an uncertain Middle East conflict and the Strait of Hormuz's effective closure. Hyperscalers are committing roughly 650 billion dollars to US AI infrastructure this year alone, and that investment assumes the supply chain holding it together remains intact, according to Moody's David Pan. The AI economy runs on tokens, tokens run on GPUs, and GPUs depend on Qatari helium, Israeli bromine, and liquefied natural gas tankers with a single, 21-mile-wide exit from the Persian Gulf. Watch for whether the conflict escalates or de-escalates next week, and whether chip manufacturers or cloud providers signal any supply chain disruptions or delays in datacenter buildouts. The issue is part of a growing pattern defining the economic conditions of the 2020s: shocks exposing the fragility of supply chains that the world took for granted. The AI buildout is the latest to be throttled by this trend, and any disruption to the flow of critical inputs could delay or derail the massive capital expenditures planned for this year.
